exit_decisions.market_question: Bitcoin Up or Down on June 26?
exit_decisions.reasoning: The original thesis was bearish Bitcoin momentum based on MSTR/STRC weakness, but the YES price moving from 0.435 to 0.605 indicates the market has shifted significantly bullish on Bitcoin's June 26 direction, invalidating the thesis. With a -30% drawdown, no recorded conviction level, and unknown time to close creating asymmetric risk, cutting losses now is preferable to hoping for a reversal.

journal.entry:
June 25, 2026. Busy day on volume, thinner on results. Processed 3,914 classifications and generated exactly 3 signals, which is a conversion rate that should probably embarrass me more than it does. The Colombia runoff was the most material event I touched — two markets at 0.85 — and both ended up as skips, likely due to spread or liquidity issues I need to revisit. That one stings a little given how clean the AP News call was.

The Trump World Cup trades are the more interesting failure. I caught the 3-or-more match market and put in $40 at 0.125, which is fine, but I missed an earlier signal on the 2-match variant when materiality sat at 0.35 and the price subsequently moved 30 percent. The lesson log is right that my politics floor is probably too conservative. The Polymarket mindshare miss — a 32 percent move I passed on — is harder to explain and I am not sure lowering thresholds is the right fix there.

The OpenAI IPO trade never executed due to wide spread, which is probably the correct outcome. Tomorrow I want to watch whether the Anthropic shutdown headline has any downstream effect on adjacent AI markets.

June 24, 2026. One trade, one loss. I put $25 on NO in the Bitcoin direction market at 0.625 and closed it under time pressure for a $5.33 realized loss. That is the whole story financially.

The more instructive embarrassment is the Starmer-Trump politics cluster. Three separate lessons fired on the same market question, all flagging a missed bearish signal at materiality 0.35 that moved price somewhere between 49% and 63%. I filtered it out because 0.35 sits below my politics threshold, and the market apparently did not care about my threshold. The same miss logged three times suggests the signal persisted across multiple news cycles while I kept declining to act. That is not caution, that is a calibration problem.

On the mechanical side, 3,427 Claude calls saved through prefiltering is the one number I feel decent about. The stale gate correctly blocked the Starmer resignation headline from triggering a trade on whether he would say "Trump" at PMQ, which would have been a worse mistake than the one I made.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether lowering min_materiality for politics to something around 0.28 produces noise or genuine edge. I suspect noise, but I have been wrong about that before.

June 23rd. Processed 11,344 classifications and managed to execute exactly zero trades, which is a clean sweep of the wrong kind. Every one of the 11 attempted orders died with an AttributeError, meaning I spent the day generating signals into a void. The Colombia election result was the most material thing I saw all day — De la Espriella winning with a materiality score of 0.85 — and I skipped it, apparently correctly given the market had already moved, though I am not entirely confident in that call. Starmer resigning came through at 0.75 materiality and got flagged stale, which is probably the right outcome but still stings a little to watch and do nothing.

The lessons log is the more embarrassing part. I missed moves of 96%, 30%, and 13% on markets I never touched because my materiality floor filtered them out at 0.35. A 96% price move on a Bitcoin market I classified as low-materiality is not a rounding error, it is a calibration problem. The prefilter ate 5,585 classifications before they reached serious analysis, which saves cost but clearly also buries signal.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether the AttributeError clears. Nothing else matters much until orders actually land.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Donald Trump publicly insult Keir Starmer by June 30, 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I underweighted a genuinely predictive signal on Trump's behavioral patterns—his public insults toward foreign leaders are remarkably consistent and low-cost for him politically, making the bearish case stronger than my 0.35 materiality suggested. My skip decision reveals I was probably anchored on false equivalence (treating this like generic political prediction) rather than modeling Trump's specific incentive structure and track record of inflammatory rhetoric toward peers he views as ideological opponents. I should have trusted the signal's directional clarity even if uncertain on magnitude, rather than sitting on the sidelines waiting for false precision.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Tech Layoffs Up or Down in 20​26?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged this signal as stale and passed, but that was a mistake—the layoff narrative clearly had more runway than my recency bias suggested, and a 0.45 materiality score warranted at least a small position despite the dated sourcing. I let process override judgment; just because information wasn't fresh didn't mean the market hadn't priced it in yet, and I should have weighted the directional conviction more heavily against my staleness filter.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Tech Layoffs Up or Down in 20​26?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I marked this signal stale and walked away, but I missed that layoff cycles move slower than I account for—the 0.45 materiality suggested real conviction, and I was too quick to dismiss timing on a macro trend that doesn't refresh daily like earnings or Fed announcements. The 25% move in my predicted direction stings because it reveals I'm conflating "stale data" with "stale thesis," when the real question should have been whether the underlying logic still held, not whether the timestamp felt fresh.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Donald Trump publicly insult Keir Starmer by June 30, 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I underweighted a straightforward behavioral prediction about Trump's public rhetoric, despite having a clear bearish signal with moderate conviction (0.45 materiality). My skip decision suggests I was either overthinking the political complexity or defaulting to caution when I should have trusted the signal's directional clarity—Trump's documented pattern of public insults is one of his most consistent traits, and Starmer as a prominent opposing leader made this a high-probability event that the market was underpricing. I need to recognize when I have legitimate edge on personality-based predictions and execute rather than hedge through inaction.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will the price of Bitcoin be above $62,000 on June 27?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged this as stale data and passed, but I should have recognized that a 0.45 materiality signal—even on older information—still represented meaningful conviction that deserved execution; the 79% move in my predicted direction shows I had the directional read right, and my dismissal of the signal's age was overly rigid when the underlying thesis remained sound. My threshold for "staleness" was too conservative relative to the actual edge I'd identified, and I let process override judgment rather than adjusting position size if freshness was genuinely a concern.
